caveats: a: BLS data are NAICS 311100, not 311111 or the narrowed contract-manufacturing lens.
a: Task exposure is inferred from occupations and documented use cases, not directly measured.
a: The estimate excludes savings from conventional automation already in place.
rho: Published examples skew toward larger and more advanced plants.
rho: The poll populations are conference or trade-publication audiences rather than a probability sample.
rho: Conventional robotics and AI-enabled software are intertwined in reported projects.
e: Private-label dollar share omits contract manufacturing for third-party branded products.
e: Firm count and revenue share differ substantially in a concentrated industry.
e: The OC&C supplier list is explicitly non-exhaustive and mixes ownership situations.
s5: The observed deal list is global and spans the wider pet-nutrition value chain.
s5: Announced deals are not a random sample and may include non-control or out-of-band transactions.
s5: Owner age and succession readiness are not directly observed.
q: No representative contract dataset was found.
q: Retention differs sharply between annual retailer tenders and longer branded co-manufacturing agreements.
q: Customer concentration can overwhelm nominal switching friction.
d5: APPA covers the whole pet industry rather than manufactured dog and cat food alone.
d5: Nominal spending and household ownership do not directly measure constant-quality food quantity.
d5: Obesity management, smaller pets, and premium calorie density could reduce tonnage despite sales growth.
o: The operator share concerns the narrowed outsourced lens, not whether food itself remains physical.
o: Large customers can vertically integrate even when regulatory accountability remains.
o: The estimate does not assume that all current co-manufacturers retain their specific customers.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B production for pet-food brands and retailers, generally priced per unit, batch, or production run under supply or co-manufacturing agreements, with formulation, packaging, quality, and compliance services embedded in the relationship.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to contract manufacturing, co-packing, and private-label production because NAICS 311111 combines outsourced manufacturers with own-brand product companies whose economics are not recurring outsourced services.
lens.excluded_activities: Own-brand manufacturers without material third-party production, captive plants, ingredient-only suppliers, distributors, retailers, financing vehicles, and businesses outside the LMM band.
lens.included_activities: Independent dog- and cat-food contract manufacturers and co-packers that repeatedly formulate, process, package, or private-label food for external brands and retailers, at roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader four-digit animal-food industry rather than 311111 contract manufacturers and reports employment rather than task exposure. I map wage-bearing work in office support, business operations, production planning, inspection, maintenance, and selected machine/packaging roles to currently exposed tasks, then discount physical handling, sanitation, driving, and already-automated process control. The broader feed-industry mix may understate the white-collar share of customer-facing pet-food co-manufacturers, biasing the estimate downward.
caveats: BLS data are NAICS 311100, not 311111 or the narrowed contract-manufacturing lens.
Task exposure is inferred from occupations and documented use cases, not directly measured.
The estimate excludes savings from conventional automation already in place.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 10-15 U.S. pet-food co-manufacturers, split by plant, quality, formulation, supply-chain, sales, and back-office roles, with current automation status and time-on-task observations.
a.rationale: The 2023 BLS mix is dominated by production (43.30%) and transportation/material moving (20.15%), while office support is 10.07%, management 6.80%, and business/financial operations 3.44%. AI can materially assist scheduling, purchasing, documentation, customer service, formulation support, inspection analytics, and predictive maintenance, but most line, sanitation, warehousing, and driving work remains physical. Pet-food examples confirm computer vision, data harvesting, predictive inspection, and robotic handling as practical use cases.

d5.bridge: APPA measures and forecasts total nominal U.S. pet-industry expenditure and pet ownership, not constant-price, constant-quality quantities of outsourced dog/cat food manufacturing. I use stable-to-growing pet ownership and the stated 2026 growth less its roughly 2% inflation component as a near-term real-demand anchor, then temper it for five years because food mix, premiumization, and channel shifts can raise dollars without equivalent physical quantity. Total pet-industry data may overstate food-manufacturing volume growth.
caveats: APPA covers the whole pet industry rather than manufactured dog and cat food alone.
Nominal spending and household ownership do not directly measure constant-quality food quantity.
Obesity management, smaller pets, and premium calorie density could reduce tonnage despite sales growth.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of Circana or NielsenIQ U.S. dog/cat food unit and equivalent-volume data by format, deflated for package-size and quality mix, combined with co-manufacturer shipment volumes and customer forecasts.
d5.rationale: APPA reports 95 million pet-owning households in 2025, dog ownership rising from 51% to 53% of households, cat ownership up 5%, and projected 2026 pet-industry growth near 4.4% with roughly 2% inflation. Food is essential and pet ownership is resilient, but value-seeking and product premiumization make spend a noisy volume proxy. The base implies modest cumulative real quantity growth rather than extrapolating the one-year nominal forecast.

e.bridge: Private-label sales share and a non-exhaustive co-manufacturer landscape measure revenue channels and named companies, not the share of frozen-band firms eligible under the lens. I treat private label plus contract production for branded clients as the eligible population and allow firm share to exceed private-label revenue share because independent co-manufacturers are typically smaller than integrated branded leaders. Using private-label sales alone would bias eligibility downward; including all firms that offer occasional co-packing would bias it upward.
caveats: Private-label dollar share omits contract manufacturing for third-party branded products.
Firm count and revenue share differ substantially in a concentrated industry.
The OC&C supplier list is explicitly non-exhaustive and mixes ownership situations.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment-to-firm census classifying 311111 companies by third-party manufacturing revenue, with eligibility defined as more than half of revenue from recurring external co-manufacturing or private-label contracts and EBITDA verified in the $1-10M band.
e.rationale: U.S. private-label pet food was only 3.3% of dog and cat food dollars in 2023, yet trade research identifies several large contract manufacturers and a fragmented set of niche co-manufacturers across dry, wet, frozen, freeze-dried, and treats. The eligible recurring outsourced-service population is therefore a meaningful minority of firms but not the industry majority.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311111 ($3,081,162,177) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311111 ($27,491,038,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $962,976 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311111); class <5: 128 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,695,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $411,065 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,659,132 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $405,518 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,314,951 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $810,530 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,513,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,518,244 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $115,825,308 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,663,359 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $892,137,458 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $136,050,962 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 52

caveats: The operator share concerns the narrowed outsourced lens, not whether food itself remains physical.
Large customers can vertically integrate even when regulatory accountability remains.
The estimate does not assume that all current co-manufacturers retain their specific customers.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans from the top 30 pet-food brands and retailers, co-manufacturing capacity announcements, and five-year shipment data separating insourced, outsourced, and direct-to-consumer production.
o.rationale: Dog and cat food remains a physical, safety-sensitive product that software cannot supply. FDA registration, CGMP, hazard analysis, preventive controls, monitoring, corrective action, verification, traceability, and reportable-food duties preserve the need for an accountable manufacturer. Some outsourced volume could be internalized by large brands or retailers, and small-batch direct production could bypass co-manufacturers, but most year-five quantity should still require an operator of this kind.

caveats: No representative contract dataset was found.
Retention differs sharply between annual retailer tenders and longer branded co-manufacturing agreements.
Customer concentration can overwhelm nominal switching friction.
q.change_evidence: A sample of 20 customer contracts and renewal histories showing pricing formula, cost-index clauses, open-book rights, productivity givebacks, term, termination rights, and gross-margin movement after documented plant-efficiency gains.
q.rationale: Per-unit and per-run co-manufacturing contracts let the operator retain labor savings until repricing, but sophisticated brands and retailers can demand annual bids, open-book costing, productivity concessions, or price reductions at renewal. Switching friction from formulation qualification, capacity, food-safety assurance, and packaging integration slows full pass-through. The base assumes meaningful sharing over five years rather than permanent retention.

rho.bridge: Industry articles document available use cases, adoption barriers, and practitioner polling, not the five-year implemented share of exposed wage opportunity. I translate demonstrated vision, inspection, maintenance, analytics, and back-office use cases into implementation potential, then haircut for data silos, trust, leadership priority, capital integration, validation, and food-safety change control. Event-attendee polls likely overrepresent technology-engaged firms, biasing implementation potential upward.
caveats: Published examples skew toward larger and more advanced plants.
The poll populations are conference or trade-publication audiences rather than a probability sample.
Conventional robotics and AI-enabled software are intertwined in reported projects.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of LMM co-manufacturers showing baseline labor hours, funded AI/automation projects, go-live dates, validation failures, and realized labor-hour reductions 12-24 months after deployment.
rho.rationale: Pet-food plants already use vision, robots, autonomous inspection, OEE data, and predictive maintenance, but 2026 practitioner polling identifies weak measurement, data quality, trust, and scaling as binding constraints. FDA-required monitoring, corrective action, verification, and recordkeeping also make unvalidated workflow replacement difficult. Over five years, about half of the exposed opportunity is implementable in a typical LMM operator, with higher realization in standardized back-office and inspection-support workflows than in food-safety decisions.

s5.bridge: Trade deal compilations and advisory landscape commentary observe transactions and fragmentation across global pet nutrition, including brands, manufacturers, and suppliers, not the five-year transfer probability for U.S. eligible LMM co-manufacturers. I use repeated manufacturer acquisitions and identified family-owned niche firms as evidence of a functioning control market, then discount for geography, deal-selection bias, strategic asset purchases, and deals outside the EBITDA band. Headline activity likely biases the estimate upward.
caveats: The observed deal list is global and spans the wider pet-nutrition value chain.
Announced deals are not a random sample and may include non-control or out-of-band transactions.
Owner age and succession readiness are not directly observed.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, CapIQ, or GF Data transaction records for 2016-2026 matched to a verified denominator of U.S. 311111 co-manufacturers in the $1-10M EBITDA band, plus owner-age and succession interviews for private firms.
s5.rationale: A 2024 trade compilation recorded 28 pet-nutrition transactions in six months and included multiple pet-food manufacturers and private-label assets; OC&C describes plentiful M&A opportunities and a fragmented co-manufacturer landscape, including family-owned niche producers. That supports a real but not annualized one-for-one transfer channel. A roughly one-in-five five-year probability is defensible, with wide uncertainty because the denominator of eligible U.S. firms is unknown.

principal_driver: Durable recurring pet-food demand combined with practical AI-assisted planning, quality, maintenance, and administrative workflows in contract manufacturing.
principal_weakness: Only a minority of 311111 firms fit the outsourced-service lens, and no representative evidence directly measures their transfer rate or savings pass-through.
